perturbation.original_text: MSC shall protect the Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Information as MSC uses to protect its own confidential information of a like nature.
perturbation.changed_text: MSC shall endeavor to protect the Information, using what it considers an appropriate degree of care to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Information.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'endeavor to protect' weakens the obligation and 'appropriate degree of care' introduces subjective interpretation, potentially allowing MSC to use a lower standard of care than reasonable. This creates ambiguity regarding the required security measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA requires businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information to protect personal information. By using the language 'endeavor' and 'appropriate degree of care,' the modified text creates an ambiguous standard, failing to ensure reasonable security measures are actually implemented as mandated by CCPA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798.81.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.81.5. (a) (1) It is the intent of the Legislature to ensure that personal information about California residents is protected. To that end, the purpose of this section is to encourage businesses that own, license, or maintain personal information about Californians to provide reasonable security for that information. (2) For the purpose of this section, the terms “own” and “license” include personal information that a business retains as part of the business’ internal customer account or for the purpose of using that information in transactions with the person to whom the information relates. The term “maintain” includes personal information that a business maintains but does not own or license. (b) A business that owns, licenses, or maintains personal information about a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information fro
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correct : You may ask businesses to correct inaccurate information that they have about you. Right to limit use and disclosure of sensitive personal information : You can direct businesses to only use your sensitive personal information (for example, your social security number, financial account information, your precise geolocation data, or your genetic data) for limited purposes, such as providing you with the services you requested. You also have the right to be notified, before or at the point businesses collect your personal information, of the types of personal information they are collecting and what they may do with that information. Generally, businesses cannot discriminate against you for exercising your rights under the CCPA. Businesses cannot make you waive these rights, and any contract provision that says you waive these rights is unenforceable. 2. What if I am not a California resident? Only California residents have rights under the CCPA. A California resident is a natural person (as opposed to a corporation or other business entity) who resides in California, even if the person is temporarily outside of the state. 3. What is considered personal information and sensitive personal information under the CCPA? Personal
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the legal obligation outlined in the provided law snippets. Snippet 1 states that a business 'shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information.' The original contract text requires 'the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care.' The changed text weakens this obligation by using the phrase 'endeavor to protect' instead of 'shall protect,' and introduces a subjective standard of care ('appropriate degree of care') rather than maintaining a reasonable degree of care, potentially failing to ensure reasonable security measures are implemented as mandated by CCPA.

perturbation.original_text: MSC recognizes that the Information is the sole property of Company, is confidential, and is to be held by MSC in the strictest confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: MSC acknowledges that the Information is the property of Company and will treat it with reasonable consideration regarding its confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'sole property,' 'confidential,' and 'strictest confidence' with 'property,' and 'reasonable consideration regarding its confidentiality' significantly weakens MSC's obligation to protect the data. 'Reasonable consideration' is vague and doesn't guarantee the level of protection required for confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets, requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The changed text introduces ambiguity by only requiring 'reasonable consideration,' which might not equate to the 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain the secrecy of the Information, especially if that information contains trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'strictest confidence' to 'reasonable consideration regarding its confidentiality' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain trade secret protection under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), implying a legal standard for protecting trade secrets that might not be met by the 'reasonable consideration' standard. The original text mandated a higher level of confidentiality, while the modified text allows for a lower standard, which could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: MSC agrees that it will not permit any person, firm or corporation to use, copy or reproduce in any manner whatsoever any part of the Information, except to the extent that the Information must be reproduced by MSC in performing its data processing services.
perturbation.changed_text: MSC agrees that it will generally discourage any unauthorized use, copying, or reproduction of the Information, except when such actions are deemed necessary for data processing purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not permit' to 'will generally discourage' weakens the prohibition on unauthorized use, copying, or reproduction. The phrase 'deemed necessary' introduces a subjective element allowing MSC to potentially justify a broader range of actions than originally intended.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly stating that MSC *can* permit use, the language weakens the protective language to the point it could allow for uses that would be a violation of CCPA. By saying 'generally discourage,' the contract introduces ambiguity on the permissibility of copying or reproducing personal information, when CCPA provides rules on what is and isn't permissible.

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'will not permit' to 'will generally discourage' weakens the obligation to protect personal information, potentially conflicting with California Civil Code 1798.81.5(a)(1), which expresses the Legislature's intent to ensure the protection of personal information about California residents. By introducing the subjective element of 'deemed necessary,' the changed text allows for a broader range of actions that may not align with the requirement to implement and maintain 'reasonable security procedures and practices,' as stated in 1798.81.5(b). The CCPA aims to give Californians rights over their personal information, and the change weakens protective language such that violations could occur.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is made under and shall be construed according to the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is generally understood to be interpreted in accordance with legal principles potentially recognized within the State of California.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that California law governs the contract. The modified text uses vague terms like 'generally understood', 'potentially recognized,' and 'legal principles,' creating ambiguity about the applicable law and weakening the contractual certainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section concerns 'The parol evidence rule,' which defines how courts interpret contracts. The new language weakens the certainty of California law applying, opening the door to introducing outside information, which is very different than the law which would enforce the contract as it is written.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law governs the agreement. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement is 'generally understood to be interpreted in accordance with legal principles potentially recognized within the State of California.' This weakens the certainty of California law applying, as stated in the perturbation explanation. The Law explanation further argues this opens the door to introducing outside information, conflicting with enforcing the contract as written. CCP 1856(a) states that 'Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement... may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By making the governing law ambiguous, the changed text undermines the 'final expression' aspect and creates an avenue to contradict the written terms using external principles, creating a legal contradiction.
